title: Composting in a pit or trench
subtitle: Burying kitchen and garden waste directly in the soil
excerpt: A compost pit or trench is the simplest of all compost systems: dig a hole, bury organic waste, cover with soil. No bin, no turning, no thermometer. The buried material breaks down over 4 to 8 weeks and improves the soil structure directly. It suits small gardens where a bin would take up too much space, or where surplus summer waste needs a home.
sourceNotes: Charles Dowding no-dig composting methods (nodiga.co.uk). Garden Organic trench composting guidance. RHS guidance on in-ground composting. Soil Association research on buried organic matter and soil biology (2020).

content.text: Pit and trench composting bypasses the bin entirely. Organic waste goes directly into the soil and the soil biology does the work. The method is quiet, smell-free, and invisible once covered. It is not suited to large volumes of material or to cooked food waste (which attracts rats), but for kitchen scraps, soft prunings, and vegetable waste from the garden, it is reliable and requires almost no management.

A pit is a fixed hole in one location. It is filled over time and then left to rest. Dig a hole 50 to 60 cm deep and 30 to 40 cm across. Add kitchen waste in layers of no more than 5 to 8 cm at a time. Cover each addition with 5 cm of soil from the pile beside the pit. When the pit is full to within 10 cm of the top, cap it with soil and leave it for 6 to 8 weeks. Then dig a new pit adjacent to the first.
What works: vegetable peelings, fruit scraps, coffee grounds, tea bags (uncoated paper), soft green prunings, annual weeds without seed heads.
What to avoid: cooked food, meat, fish, dairy (attracts rats), perennial weed roots (they survive burial).

Trench composting is used in vegetable beds where the compost feeds the next year's crops. Dig a trench 30 cm deep along one end of the bed, the full width of the bed. Through autumn and winter, add kitchen waste to the trench and cover with the excavated soil. In spring, plant directly over the filled trench. Runner beans and courgettes planted over a filled trench in May get a sustained feed as the material breaks down.

Divide the vegetable plot into three or four sections. Each year, one section gets the trench. The following year's crops on that section benefit from the improved soil. This means the compost is always applied one season ahead of the plants that most need it (brassicas and root crops need open ground; legumes and courgettes tolerate partially broken-down material).

Cover each layer with 5 cm of soil immediately. Do not leave food waste exposed overnight. Cooked food and meat should not go into a trench or pit: use a sealed compost bin or caddy instead. If rats are already active in the garden, avoid pit composting until the problem is controlled.
